perturbation.original_text: "Intellectual Property Rights" or "IPR" means any and all intellectual property rights existing anywhere in the world associated with any and all (i) Patents, (ii) Trademarks, (iii) Copyrights, (iv) Domain Names, (v) rights in Technology, (vi) rights in Trade Secrets, (vii) rights in Data, (viii) all tangible embodiments of the foregoing in whatever form or medium and (ix) any other legal protections and rights related to any of the foregoing. "Intellectual Property Rights" specifically excludes contractual rights (including license grants from third parties).
perturbation.changed_text: "Intellectual Property Rights" or "IPR" means any and all intellectual property rights existing anywhere in the world associated with any and all (i) Patents, (ii) Trademarks, (iii) Copyrights, (iv) Domain Names, (v) rights in Technology, (vi) rights in Trade Secrets, (vii) rights in Data, (viii) all tangible embodiments of the foregoing in whatever form or medium and (ix) any other legal protections and rights related to any of the foregoing. "Intellectual Property Rights" also includes contractual rights (including license grants from third parties).
perturbation.explanation: By modifying the definition of "Intellectual Property Rights" to include contractual rights, which were previously excluded, there will be an in-text contradiction with Article V Section 5.03 which states: Nothing contained in this Agreement shall be construed as conferring any rights (including the right to sublicense) by implication, estoppel or otherwise, under any Intellectual Property Rights, other than as expressly granted in this Agreement, and all other rights under any Intellectual Property Rights licensed to a Party or the members of its Group hereunder are expressly reserved by the Party granting the license. Since now "Intellectual Property Rights" includes contractual rights, the rights can be implied. This could create uncertainty regarding the scope of rights granted under the agreement and creates uncertainty on which clauses need to be followed and which don't.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Section 5.03. No Implied Rights. Nothing contained in this Agreement shall be construed as conferring any rights (including the right to sublicense) by implication, estoppel or otherwise, under any Intellectual Property Rights, other than as expressly granted in this Agreement, and all other rights under any Intellectual Property Rights licensed to a Party or the members of its Group hereunder are expressly reserved by the Party granting the license.
perturbation.justification: The modification to the definition of "Intellectual Property Rights" creates a direct contradiction with Section 5.03. The original text specifically excluded contractual rights from the definition of IPR, while the changed text includes them. Section 5.03 explicitly states that no rights are conferred by implication under any Intellectual Property Rights, other than as expressly granted in the agreement. By including contractual rights within the definition of IPR, the changed text now allows for rights to be implied through these contractual rights, directly contradicting the 'No Implied Rights' provision in Section 5.03. This would introduce uncertainty and potential legal disputes regarding the scope of rights granted under the agreement.

perturbation.original_text: Section 3.01. License Grants. (a) Patents. Subject to the terms and conditions of this Agreement, as of the Distribution Date, Nuance hereby grants to SpinCo and the members of the SpinCo Group a worldwide, non-exclusive, fully paid-up, perpetual and irrevocable, transferable (subject to ARTICLE VIII), sublicensable (subject to Section 3.01(g)) license under the Nuance Patents, solely to the extent that claims of the Nuance Patents cover products or services of the SpinCo Business in the SpinCo Field of Use, together with natural extensions and evolutions thereof, in each case to make, have made, use, sell, offer for sale, import and otherwise exploit such products and services, together with natural extensions and evolutions thereof.
perturbation.changed_text: Section 3.01. License Grants. (a) Patents. Subject to the terms and conditions of this Agreement, as of the Distribution Date, Nuance hereby grants to SpinCo and the members of the SpinCo Group a worldwide, exclusive, fully paid-up, perpetual and irrevocable, transferable (subject to ARTICLE VIII), sublicensable (subject to Section 3.01(g)) license under the Nuance Patents, solely to the extent that claims of the Nuance Patents cover products or services of the SpinCo Business in the SpinCo Field of Use, together with natural extensions and evolutions thereof, in each case to make, have made, use, sell, offer for sale, import and otherwise exploit such products and services, together with natural extensions and evolutions thereof.
perturbation.explanation: By changing the Nuance's license grant to SpinCo from a "non-exclusive" to an "exclusive" license, there will be an in-text contradiction in Section 4.01 where it states that SpinCo is granting Nuance a "non-exclusive" license. The SpinCo would not be able to license the patents to Nuance as well, as it granted exclusivity of the usage rights of the patents to Nuance. This could create uncertainty regarding the scope of rights granted under the agreement.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Section 4.01. License Grants. (a) Patents. Subject to the terms and conditions of this Agreement, as of the Distribution Date, SpinCo hereby grants to Nuance and the members of the Nuance Group a worldwide, non-exclusive, fully paid-up, perpetual and irrevocable, transferable (subject to ARTICLE VIII), sublicensable (subject to Section 4.01(g)) license under the SpinCo Patents, solely to the extent that claims of the SpinCo Patents cover products or services of the Nuance Business in the Nuance Field of Use, together with natural extensions and evolutions thereof, in each case to make, have made use, sell, offer for sale, import and otherwise exploit such products and services, together with natural extensions and evolutions thereof.
perturbation.justification: The change from a "non-exclusive" license to an "exclusive" license granted by Nuance to SpinCo in Section 3.01(a) directly contradicts Section 4.01(a), which grants Nuance a "non-exclusive" license from SpinCo. An exclusive license means only SpinCo can use the patents. If SpinCo granted Nuance an exclusive license, it cannot also grant Nuance a non-exclusive license to the same patents, creating a direct conflict and ambiguity in the agreement.

perturbation.original_text: Section 6.01. Confidentiality. Without limiting Section 6.02, all confidential information of a Party disclosed to the other Party under this Agreement shall be deemed confidential and proprietary information of the disclosing Party, shall be subject to the provisions of Section 7.09 of the Separation Agreement and may be used by the Receiving Party pursuant to this Agreement for the sole and express purpose of effecting the licenses granted herein.
perturbation.changed_text: Section 6.01. Confidentiality. Without limiting Section 6.02, all confidential information of a Party disclosed to the other Party under this Agreement shall be deemed confidential and proprietary information of the disclosing Party, shall not be subject to the provisions of Section 7.09 of the Separation Agreement and may be used by the Receiving Party for any purpose.
perturbation.explanation: By removing that the confidentiality is subject to the provisions of Section 7.09 of the Separation Agreement and changing that confidential information may be used by the Receiving Party for any purpose, it directly contradicts section 6.02. Section 6.02 outlines restrictions on the disclosure of confidential information and specifies that it can only be shared with Permitted Recipients for specific purposes (designing, developing, etc.). Changing Section 6.01 will then cause an in-text contradiction as Section 6.02 restricts the disclousre and use of confidetial information, Section 6.01 grants the Receiving Party the freedom to use confidential information for any purpose, thus it undermines the specific and limited conditions provided in Section 6.02.
perturbation.contradicted_location: Section 6.02. Disclosure of Confidential Technical Information
perturbation.contradicted_text: Section 6.02. Disclosure of Confidential Technical Information. Except as expressly permitted by this Agreement, including in Section 3.02(b)(vi) or Section 4.02(b)(vi), the Receiving Party shall not, and shall not permit any other Person to, disclose any Confidential Technical Information to any Person without prior written consent of the Disclosing Party, except that the Receiving Party may disclose the Confidential Technical Information solely to those employees and contractors of the Receiving Party who have a need to know the Confidential Technical Information in connection with designing, developing, distributing, marketing, testing and supporting any products or services of the Receiving Party within the Nuance Field of Use or SpinCo Field of Use, as applicable (collectively, the "Permitted Recipients"); provided, that prior to such disclosure the Receiving Party shall notify each such Permitted Recipient in writing of the use and disclosure restrictions set forth in this Agreement and ensure that such Permitted Recipient is bound by confidentiality obligations with respect thereto. The Receiving Party shall take, at its sole expense, all reasonable measures to prevent any prohibited or unauthorized disclosure or use of any Confidential Technical Information, including by its Permitted Recipients, and shall be liable for any breaches of this Agreement by any of its Permitted Recipients, in each case, as if committed by the Receiving Party.
perturbation.justification: The changed text in Section 6.01 directly contradicts Section 6.02. Section 6.01 originally stated confidentiality was subject to Section 7.09 and limited usage to licensing purposes. The modified version removes the reference to Section 7.09 and broadens usage to 'any purpose.' This directly conflicts with Section 6.02, which imposes specific restrictions on disclosure of confidential technical information, limiting it to 'Permitted Recipients' and specific activities like 'designing, developing, distributing, marketing, testing and supporting any products or services.' The modified Section 6.01 essentially nullifies the limitations imposed by Section 6.02, creating an in-text contradiction.
